hero.headline: Since 1989.
hero.sub: We build software that works. Sometimes software that surprises.

events.text: Zdzisław Krawczyk returns from Libya. Thirty months on a construction contract in Tripoli. That's how you earned serious money back then. Enough dollars to try something of his own.
Poland is born. So are we.
We pivot to office software. Nobody is excited.
We survive Y2K. The millennium bug. The payroll system too. As did the bugs in the wall panelling...
The printer incident. Details sealed by mutual agreement.
We buy a ping-pong table. Nobody plays ping-pong. Should have bought a foosball table...
Marta joins. As cover. She doesn't know it yet, but she'll stay.
Pandemic. We already worked remotely, so essentially nothing happened.
Hania builds Skill Issue on a Friday afternoon.
Bożenka notices. "Hm. This is actually good."
Julka joins. Skill Issue goes live.

bozena.name: Bożenka from HR
bozena.title: Head of HR & Administration
bozena.bio: Has outlasted four CEOs, two office moves, and the printer incident. Does not accept CVs with the word "passionate". Does not use the word "passionate".
bozena.quote: CEOs come and go. I stay.

marta.name: Marta
marta.title: Head of Product
marta.bio: Was supposed to stay two years. She's still here. Makes roadmaps, manages clients, runs Secret Santa with a spreadsheet that has conditional formatting.

julka.name: Julka from HR
julka.title: HR Specialist
julka.bio: Joined straight from university. Has already reorganised three processes. Runs a Notion board nobody uses. Has a doc called "suggestions (please read)" — Marta has opened it twice.

zdzislaw.name: Zdzisław Krawczyk
zdzislaw.title: Founder
zdzislaw.bio: Earned it in Libya, came back with an idea. Founded Codexpol in 1989 — a computer company, whatever that meant at the time. Office software came gradually, in the mid-90s. Handed the company to Mariusz in 2018. Still asks how it's going sometimes. Mariusz says he'll call back.
zdzislaw.quote: Had to start somewhere. I had dollars and wanted to work in computers. Turned out differently, but it works.

mariusz.name: Mariusz Krawczyk
mariusz.title: CEO
mariusz.bio: Son of Zdzisław. Took over in 2018. MBA from Koźmiński. In 2019 proposed rebranding to "Codeex". Bożenka didn't update the letterhead. The matter was dropped. Has worked hybrid since 2021 — from which country varies. Recently from Bali. Shows up at the Christmas party, in the email footer photo, and when something needs signing that Bożenka can't sign herself. Which happens less and less.

hania.name: Hania Sikorska
hania.title: Mid-level Developer
hania.bio: Studied bioinformatics. Joined as a junior, quiet in meetings, active in pull requests. On a Friday afternoon in 2024 she built a CV-based job listing scraper. Told nobody. Bożenka found it on Monday.
hania.quote: Hania is still water. She carves the banks.

tomek.name: Tomek
tomek.title: Tech Lead
tomek.bio: Started as a senior developer, became tech lead because nobody else wanted to run the 9:15 standups. Knows every line of legacy code. Understands some of them. Wears t-shirts with jokes only programmers find funny — and is proud of it.

ewa.name: Ewa
ewa.title: Senior Fullstack Engineer
ewa.bio: Writes the backend before others finish their coffee, fixes the frontend before anyone opens a ticket. Treats code review as a contact sport. Her comments are short, precise, and mildly terrifying.
ewa.quote: Refactor is not a swear word.

filip.name: Filip
filip.title: Frontend Developer
filip.bio: The only person in the office with opinions about typography, spacing, and why that shade of blue is wrong. Also does UX, because someone has to. Tomek says it's not his job. Filip disagrees.
filip.quote: Pixel perfect isn't an obsession. It's a standard.

damian.name: Damian
damian.title: Data Analyst
damian.bio: Turns data into charts, charts into insights, insights into presentations. Knows exactly what the numbers say. That's precisely the problem.
damian.quote: Data doesn't lie. People lie. Data shows how.

tatiana.name: Tatiana
tatiana.title: Tester
tatiana.bio: Finds bugs in things that "definitely work". Her test list has more items than Filip's wishlist for the next sprint. Even Ewa and Tomek are afraid of her Slack messages.
tatiana.quote: Bugs don't sleep. Neither do I.

office.label: Office software
office.text: We've been doing this for 30 years. It's stable, it works, our clients are satisfied. Which clients? They'd prefer not to be named.

skillIssue.label: Skill Issue
skillIssue.text: Hania built it on a Friday. Bożenka said we're taking it. Marta made a roadmap. Julka has feedback.

origin.title: How it started
origin.p1: Hania studied bioinformatics and joined Codexpol in 2022. Quiet in meetings. Opens PRs that make people ask: "when did she even do that?"
origin.p2: On a Friday afternoon in 2024, tired of watching her friends send CVs into the void and get nothing back, she built something. It was supposed to be a job listing scraper, so she told nobody.
origin.p3: Bożenka found it on Monday, in the internal tools folder, looking for the leave request system.
origin.quote: Hania is still water. She carves the banks.
origin.attribution: — Bożenka from HR, after Hania showed her Skill Issue

footer.copy: Codexpol Sp. z o.o. © 1989–2026. All rights reserved. Especially the ones from the 90s...

homeAbout.p1: Office software since 1989. Poland changed governments, regulations, and centuries — we just kept coding.
homeAbout.p2: Today we also do network security, AI tooling, and a few things clients would rather we didn't mention.

teamStrip.label: People
teamStrip.kadrySub: Bożenka, Marta and Julka. They keep the order, the processes, and make sure nobody forgets to book their leave.
teamStrip.techSub: Tomek, Ewa, Hania, Filip, Damian and Tatiana. They write code, analyse data and find bugs — especially Tatiana.
teamStrip.zarzadSub: Zdzisław founded it. Mariusz continues. Recently from Bali.

soon.label: Coming soon
soon.title: Skill Issue
soon.tagline: Your CV as a recruiter, ATS, and the job market actually see it.
features: ATS score with specific improvement tips
Matched job offers from Polish and international boards

products.title: Products
products.subtitle: Office software since 1989. Stable, maintained, used.
products.secret: We make other things too. Some clients prefer we don't mention them.

sigmaFk.name: SIGMA FK
sigmaFk.tagline: Counting VAT since 1995. More tax code changes than any accountant wants to remember.
sigmaFk.desc: Codexpol's legendary financial and accounting system, which has survived more VAT changes than the average accountant cares to remember. Stable, comprehensive, and launched every morning at 7:59 by accounting departments across Poland.

hermes.name: HERMES Handel Magazyn
hermes.tagline: Survived three warehouse renovations and one brother-in-law from IT.
hermes.desc: A sales and warehouse system that has outlasted three warehouse renovations, five computer replacements, and one brother-in-law "from IT". Still runs fast even on a machine with an Intel Inside sticker from 2006.

teczka95.name: TECZKA 95
teczka95.tagline: The name stayed in 1995. Clients are used to it.
teczka95.desc: Document and personnel file archiving for companies with binders older than some of their employees. The name stayed in 1995 and nobody sees a reason to change it, because "clients are used to it".

komBit.name: KOM-BIT Kadry i Płace
komBit.tagline: Everything HR is afraid to touch manually in Excel.
komBit.desc: HR, payroll, ZUS contributions, and leave management in one place. Particularly popular with accounting offices and people who have been clicking here for fifteen years and need it to work.

vatowiec.name: VATOWIEC
vatowiec.tagline: Appears whenever the Finance Ministry changes something "just slightly" again.
vatowiec.desc: VAT and JPK declaration software. A small module with enormous psychological support power for accounting departments. Updated after every regulatory change. There are many.

zapora.name: ZAPORA
zapora.tagline: Stands between your infrastructure and several thousand servers with bad intentions.
zapora.desc: Poland's first dedicated anti-DDoS system. Handles volumetric attacks, SYN floods, and other things whose names sound worse than they are. Bożenka thinks it's "some security program". She's not wrong.

legacy.name: LEGACY
legacy.tagline: Tomek understood it. Tomek is on leave. Now the AI understands it.
legacy.desc: An AI-powered tool for analysing, documenting, and migrating legacy code to modern technologies. Works with COBOL, VB6, Delphi, 'some Perl from 2003', and several other things you're running in production and prefer not to discuss. Legacy doesn't ask why the code looks the way it does. It just reads it.

games.title: Gamedev. We tried.
games.subtitle: Two games. One year. Thirty-four copies.
games.epilogue: After these results we decided to focus on office software. It was the right call.

podroz.name: BUSINESS TRIP
podroz.tagline: Zenon Wróblewski. Logistics clerk. Business trip to Katowice. The invoice is wrong.
podroz.desc: You play a delegate who must sign an incorrect invoice in Katowice. The game starts on the platform at 5:42. It ends when you file your expense report. There is no way to speed this up.
podroz.review: Technically correct. We don't understand the point.

wczasy.name: COMPANY HOLIDAY
wczasy.tagline: A week at FWP Ustroń resort. Pool closed since 1988. Breakfast 7:00–7:45.
wczasy.desc: Seven days at a workers' holiday resort. The neighbour in room 304 plays cards until 11pm. The pool is closed. The optional castle trip — the coach was late. Three endings. None are good.
wczasy.review: We don't understand why they released a second one.

about.title: About
about.sub: Office software since 1989. Poland, governments, economic transformations — we just kept coding.
